content: Drug tolerance is a diminished response to a drug following repeated administration, requiring higher doses to achieve the same effect. Mechanisms include: pharmacokinetic tolerance (increased metabolism via enzyme induction - alcohol, barbiturates); pharmacodynamic tolerance (receptor downregulation/desensitization - opioids, benzodiazepines); tachyphylaxis (rapid tolerance after few doses - nitrates, amphetamines). Cross-tolerance occurs between drugs with similar mechanisms (benzodiazepines and alcohol both act on GABA receptors). Tolerance develops at different rates for different effects: opioid tolerance to analgesia and euphoria develops quickly; tolerance to constipation and miosis develops minimally. Reverse tolerance (sensitization) occurs with some drugs (alcohol neurotoxicity, stimulant psychosis).
